hy: Normal sun-exposed skin already harbors clonal oncogenic-driver mutations.
src: Bologna Ch 107, "Gradual Cancer Evolution Versus Punctuated Evolution"

stem: In cancer genomes, copy-number changes typically take which tumor-type-specific pattern?

options.A: Amplification of regions encoding oncogenes (e.g. BRAF, MYC, cyclins) and deletion of regions encoding tumor suppressors (e.g. TP53, CDKN2A)
options.B: Amplification of tumor suppressors and deletion of oncogenes
options.C: Uniform loss of every chromosome arm
options.D: No recurrent pattern; copy number is random across tumors

exp: Copy-number alterations accumulate in tumor-type-specific patterns, most notably amplification of regions encoding oncogenes (e.g. BRAF, MYC, cyclins) and deletion of regions encoding tumor suppressors (e.g. TP53, CDKN2A). Option B inverts this, C (uniform arm loss) and D (no pattern) are incorrect. These recurrent gains/losses are themselves evidence of selection.
hy: Cancers amplify oncogenes (BRAF/MYC/cyclins) and delete tumor suppressors (TP53/CDKN2A).
src: Bologna Ch 107, "The Darwinian Model of Gradual Cancer Evolution"

stem: Which two growth-factor signaling pathways are the prominent oncogenic proliferative pathways highlighted in the chapter?

options.A: The RTK–RAS–MAPK pathway and the PI3K–AKT–mTOR pathway
options.B: The clotting cascade and the complement pathway
options.C: The Krebs cycle and oxidative phosphorylation
options.D: The renin–angiotensin and natriuretic-peptide pathways

exp: The chapter highlights the RTK–RAS–MAPK and PI3K–AKT–mTOR growth-factor signaling pathways as the prominent oncogenic proliferative pathways (alongside p53–Rb cell-cycle control and DNA-damage responses). The clotting/complement (B), Krebs/oxidative phosphorylation (C), and renin–angiotensin/natriuretic (D) pathways are not the proliferative oncogenic pathways described.
hy: Proliferative oncogenic pathways: RTK–RAS–MAPK and PI3K–AKT–mTOR.

stem: How do oncogenic RAS mutations alter RAS protein function?

options.A: They impair the GTPase activity of RAS, locking it in a constitutively active, GTP-bound state that signals independently of upstream growth-factor receptors
options.B: They lock RAS in an inactive GDP-bound state
options.C: They convert RAS into a tumor suppressor
options.D: They prevent RAS from binding GTP at all, silencing the pathway

exp: Oncogenic RAS mutations impair the intrinsic GTPase activity of RAS, locking it in a constitutively active, GTP-bound state that signals downstream (MAPK cascade) independently of upstream receptor activation. They do not lock it in the inactive GDP-bound state (B), do not convert it to a tumor suppressor (C), and do not abolish GTP binding (D). RAS is a binary on/off switch coupling receptor tyrosine kinases to growth responses.
hy: Oncogenic RAS = GTPase-dead → constitutively GTP-bound "on."

stem: What is the "Warburg effect" referenced in the chapter?

options.A: Aerobic glycolysis — conversion of glucose to lactate even under oxygen-rich conditions — providing energy and biosynthetic building blocks for proliferating cancer cells
options.B: The complete shutdown of glucose uptake by tumor cells
options.C: Exclusive reliance on mitochondrial oxidative phosphorylation
options.D: The loss of all metabolic activity in senescent cells

exp: The Warburg effect is aerobic glycolysis — the conversion of glucose to lactate even under oxygen-rich conditions — which supplies both energy and biosynthetic building blocks (for RNA, DNA, protein, and lipids) needed for proliferation. It is not a shutdown of glucose uptake (B), not exclusive oxidative phosphorylation (C), and not loss of metabolic activity (D). BRAF inhibitors can abrogate tumor glucose uptake seen on PET-CT.
hy: Warburg effect = aerobic glycolysis (glucose → lactate) fueling biosynthesis.

stem: Why is p53 termed the "guardian of the genome," and how common is its inactivation?

options.A: It senses irreparable DNA damage and triggers senescence or programmed cell death; loss-of-function TP53 mutations are the single most frequent oncogenic alteration across tumor types
options.B: It directly synthesizes new DNA; TP53 mutations are extremely rare
options.C: It is an oncogene whose amplification drives most cancers
options.D: It only functions in keratinocytes and has no role in other tissues

exp: p53 is the "guardian of the genome" because it senses irreparable DNA damage and initiates programmed senescence or cell death; loss-of-function TP53 mutations are the single most frequent oncogenic alteration across multiple tumor types. It does not synthesize DNA (B), is a tumor suppressor not an oncogene (C), and functions in essentially all tissues (D).
hy: p53 = guardian of the genome; TP53 loss is the most common oncogenic alteration.
src: Bologna Ch 107, "Mutations in p53 and Rb Tumor Suppressor Pathways"

stem: Which molecular relationships at the G1/S checkpoint are correct?

options.A: Growth signals drive Cyclin D–CDK4/6 activity that phosphorylates Rb (releasing E2F); p16 (a CDKN2A product) inhibits CDK4/6 to enforce the checkpoint
options.B: p16 activates CDK4/6 to push cells through the checkpoint
options.C: Rb phosphorylation arrests the cell cycle by sequestering E2F
options.D: Cyclin D inhibits cell-cycle entry

exp: Growth signals drive Cyclin D–CDK4/6, which phosphorylates Rb to release E2F and permit S-phase entry; p16 (a CDKN2A product) inhibits CDK4/6 to enforce the G1/S checkpoint. p16 does not activate CDK4/6 (B); Rb phosphorylation releases rather than sequesters E2F (C); and Cyclin D promotes, not inhibits, cell-cycle entry (D).
hy: Cyclin D–CDK4/6 → phosphorylate Rb → release E2F; p16 (CDKN2A) brakes CDK4/6.

stem: Which tumor-suppressor pathways regulate cellular senescence, and what is the significance of elevated p53/p16 in preneoplastic cells?

options.A: The p14ARF/MDM2/p53 and p16/pRb pathways; increased p53 and p16 expression are hallmarks of preneoplastic cells and represent a barrier to cancer development
options.B: The Hedgehog and Notch pathways; elevated p53/p16 promote malignant transformation
options.C: The RAS–MAPK pathway alone; p53/p16 are irrelevant to senescence
options.D: Senescence is unregulated and not a barrier to cancer

exp: Senescence is regulated by the p14ARF/MDM2/p53 and p16/pRb tumor-suppressor pathways, and increased p53 and p16 expression are hallmarks of preneoplastic cells that act as a barrier to malignant progression — inactivating these pathways allows progression. Hedgehog/Notch (B) are developmental, not the senescence axis; RAS–MAPK alone (C) does not regulate senescence; and senescence is a regulated barrier (D is wrong).
hy: Senescence via p14ARF/MDM2/p53 + p16/pRb; high p53/p16 = preneoplastic barrier.

stem: The CDKN2A locus encodes which protein product(s)?

options.A: Both p16 and p14ARF — feeding the Rb and p53 pathways, respectively
options.B: Only BRAF
options.C: Only the Rb protein
options.D: Cyclin D and CDK4

exp: The CDKN2A locus encodes two distinct tumor-suppressor proteins — p16 (which inhibits CDK4/6, feeding the Rb pathway) and p14ARF (which stabilizes p53 by inhibiting MDM2). It does not encode BRAF (B), the Rb protein (C), or Cyclin D/CDK4 (D). This is why CDKN2A loss simultaneously cripples both the Rb and p53 axes.
hy: CDKN2A encodes p16 (→Rb) AND p14ARF (→p53).
src: Bologna Ch 107, "Mutations in p53 and Rb Tumor Suppressor Pathways" / Fig 107.4

stem: How does loss of p53 function impair apoptosis, and which caspases initiate the cascade?

options.A: Without p53, the pro-apoptotic protein Bax is inhibited, impairing apoptosis; the cascade begins with initiator caspases 8 and 9 that activate effector caspases
options.B: Loss of p53 hyperactivates Bax, causing excessive apoptosis
options.C: Apoptosis is initiated by caspase 3 alone, independent of p53
options.D: p53 loss has no effect on the apoptotic machinery

exp: In the absence of p53, the pro-apoptotic protein Bax is inhibited, impairing apoptosis and favoring cell survival; apoptosis normally begins with initiator caspases 8 and 9, which activate downstream effector caspases, nucleases, and lytic enzymes. Loss of p53 does not hyperactivate Bax (B), the cascade is not caspase-3-only (C), and p53 loss clearly affects apoptosis (D).
hy: p53 loss → Bax inhibited → impaired apoptosis; initiators are caspases 8 and 9.

stem: Besides classic apoptosis, which newer programmed cell-death pathways does the chapter list?

options.A: Ferroptosis, necroptosis, and pyroptosis
options.B: Mitosis, meiosis, and cytokinesis
options.C: Glycolysis, lipolysis, and proteolysis
options.D: Senescence, quiescence, and differentiation only

exp: The chapter notes that, beyond classic apoptosis, several newer programmed cell-death pathways have been described — ferroptosis, necroptosis, and pyroptosis — each with different effects on surrounding tissue. The other options list cell-division stages (B), metabolic processes (C), or growth-arrest states (D) rather than death pathways.
hy: Newer death pathways: ferroptosis, necroptosis, pyroptosis.

stem: Which event is described as a frequent early step that predisposes cells with defective cell-cycle control to chromosomal instability?

options.A: Whole-genome doubling, leading to aneuploidy and progressive chromosomal instability
options.B: Complete loss of all mitochondria
options.C: Permanent G0 arrest
options.D: Restoration of the p53–Rb network

exp: Whole-genome doubling is described as a frequent early event that predisposes cells with defective cell-cycle control to acquire further chromosomal alterations and ultimately chromosomal instability (aneuploidy). Loss of all mitochondria (B), permanent G0 arrest (C), and restoration of the p53–Rb network (D) would not drive instability.
hy: Whole-genome doubling is an early step toward aneuploidy/chromosomal instability.

stem: What is the role of the cGAS–STING pathway in cancer cells with chromosomal instability?

options.A: It senses cytosolic DNA (from ruptured micronuclei) and produces interferons; early it alerts innate immunity, but later it predominantly drives proinflammatory responses that support immune evasion and metastasis
options.B: It directly repairs double-strand DNA breaks
options.C: It is a growth-factor receptor that activates RAS
options.D: It silences all immune responses to the tumor

exp: The cGAS–STING pathway senses cytosolic DNA leaking from ruptured micronuclei in aneuploid cells and produces interferons; early in cancer evolution this alerts innate immunity, but later it predominantly drives proinflammatory transcriptional responses that support immune evasion and metastatic dissemination. It does not repair DNA (B), is not a growth-factor receptor (C), and does not globally silence anti-tumor immunity (D).
hy: cGAS–STING senses cytosolic DNA → interferons; later fosters immune evasion/metastasis.

stem: The chapter likens advanced cancer tissue to what, because of its inflammation, neovascularization, and altered matrix?

options.A: A chronic wound that does not heal
options.B: A normally regenerating epidermis
options.C: An inert scar with no cellular activity
options.D: A fully differentiated terminal tissue

exp: Because advanced cancer tissue shows persistent inflammation, neovascularization, and altered extracellular matrix — features also seen in wound healing — the chapter describes cancer as behaving like a chronic wound that does not heal. It is not normal regenerating epidermis (B), an inert scar (C), or fully differentiated terminal tissue (D).
hy: Cancer behaves like a "chronic wound that does not heal."

stem: In normal Hedgehog signaling and its activation, which statement is correct?

options.A: Unbound Patched (PTCH1) silences Smoothened (SMO); Hedgehog binding to Patched relieves this repression, allowing SMO to signal via GLI to the nucleus
options.B: Unbound Patched activates SMO; Hedgehog binding silences the pathway
options.C: SMO is a transcription factor that directly binds DNA without GLI
options.D: GLI inhibits Patched to block the pathway

exp: In normal Hedgehog signaling, unbound Patched (PTCH1) silences Smoothened (SMO); when Hedgehog ligand binds Patched, the repression of SMO is relieved, allowing SMO to transduce signals via GLI transcription factors to the nucleus. Option B inverts the logic, C is wrong because SMO signals through GLI rather than binding DNA itself, and D reverses the GLI–Patched relationship.
hy: Patched normally silences SMO; Hedgehog binding releases SMO → GLI.

stem: How does PTCH1 loss-of-function cause constitutive Hedgehog signaling, and what activates the pathway in sporadic BCCs with intact PTCH1?

options.A: Inactivating PTCH1 mutations produce dysfunctional Patched that cannot repress SMO (mimicking ligand binding), driving constitutive GLI activity; sporadic BCCs with intact PTCH1 instead carry activating SMO mutations
options.B: PTCH1 loss silences SMO; sporadic BCCs are driven by PTCH1 amplification
options.C: PTCH1 is an oncogene whose activation drives BCC
options.D: Sporadic BCCs are driven by GLI deletions

exp: Inactivating PTCH1 mutations yield dysfunctional Patched that can no longer repress Smoothened — mimicking constant ligand binding — so SMO and GLI are constitutively active; sporadic BCCs that retain intact PTCH1 instead acquire activating SMO mutations to achieve the same constitutive signaling. PTCH1 loss does not silence SMO (B), PTCH1 is a tumor suppressor not an oncogene (C), and sporadic BCCs are driven by SMO activation rather than GLI deletion (D).
hy: PTCH1 loss-of-function or SMO gain-of-function → constitutive Hedgehog in BCC.

stem: Vismodegib and sonidegib treat BCC by which mechanism, and what limits their durability?

options.A: They inhibit Smoothened (SMO); at least 50% of advanced BCCs develop resistance, commonly by acquiring SMO mutations
options.B: They inhibit GLI directly; resistance never develops
options.C: They are PTCH1 agonists that restore Patched function
options.D: They block VEGFR2 to starve the tumor of blood supply

exp: Vismodegib and sonidegib are Smoothened (SMO) inhibitors used for basal cell nevus syndrome and locally advanced or metastatic BCC; at least 50% of advanced BCCs develop resistance, commonly by acquiring SMO mutations. They do not inhibit GLI directly (B), are not PTCH1 agonists (C), and are not anti-angiogenic VEGFR2 blockers (D).
hy: Vismodegib/sonidegib = SMO inhibitors; ≥50% advanced BCC resist via SMO mutations.

stem: In normal skin and embryogenesis, Hedgehog signaling is responsible for what, and oncogenic Hedgehog activation is seen in which other cancers?

options.A: Developmental regulation of hair follicles and sebaceous glands (and stem-cell maintenance/repair); oncogenic activation occurs in BCC, medulloblastoma, and pancreatic, breast, colon, ovarian, and small cell lung cancers
options.B: Only melanocyte pigmentation; oncogenic activation is unique to BCC
options.C: Sweat-gland secretion only; it has no role in other cancers
options.D: Nail-plate formation only

exp: In skin, Hedgehog signaling governs the developmental regulation of hair follicles and sebaceous glands (and contributes to epithelial/neural stem-cell maintenance and tissue repair); oncogenic Hedgehog activation is seen not only in BCC but also in medulloblastoma and pancreatic, breast, colon, ovarian, and small cell lung carcinomas. Restricting it to pigmentation (B), sweat-gland secretion (C), or nail formation (D) is incorrect.
hy: Hedgehog → hair follicles/sebaceous glands; oncogenic in BCC, medulloblastoma, and several visceral cancers.

stem: Immune checkpoint blockade — a breakthrough in cancer immunotherapy — targets which receptors on T cells?

options.A: CTLA-4 and PD-1
options.B: VEGFR2 and EGFR
options.C: PTCH1 and SMO
options.D: Caspase 8 and caspase 9

exp: Immune checkpoint blockade — the breakthrough of modern T-cell immunotherapy — uses monoclonal antibodies against the immunoregulatory receptors CTLA-4 and PD-1 on T cells. VEGFR2/EGFR (B) are angiogenesis/growth receptors, PTCH1/SMO (C) are Hedgehog components, and caspases 8/9 (D) are apoptotic enzymes.
hy: Checkpoint blockade targets CTLA-4 and PD-1.

stem: Why does the efficacy of T-cell immunotherapy correlate with a tumor's mutational burden?

options.A: Mutated peptides (neoantigens) are presented to T cells on MHC molecules like viral antigens, so a higher mutational burden generates more neoantigens for T cells to recognize
options.B: A higher mutational burden makes tumor cells invisible to T cells
options.C: Mutational burden has no relationship to immunotherapy response
options.D: Neoantigens are presented only on red blood cells

exp: Mutated peptides — neoantigens — arise stochastically from genomic alterations and are presented to T cells on MHC molecules just like viral antigens; therefore a higher mutational burden yields more neoantigens and greater opportunity for T-cell recognition, explaining the correlation between tumor mutational burden and immunotherapy efficacy. A high burden does not hide cells from T cells (B), the correlation is real (C), and neoantigens are presented on nucleated cells' MHC, not red cells (D).
hy: More mutations → more neoantigens on MHC → better immunotherapy response.

stem: Which mechanisms do cancer cells use to evade T-cell immunity?

options.A: Transcriptional downregulation of antigen-presentation genes, acquisition of dedifferentiated stem-like phenotypes, and loss-of-function mutations in MHC molecules
options.B: Overexpression of MHC and neoantigens to attract T cells
options.C: Increased antigen presentation to enhance recognition
options.D: Permanent entry into apoptosis

exp: Cancer cells evade T cells by transcriptionally downregulating antigen-presentation genes, adopting dedifferentiated stem-like phenotypes, and acquiring loss-of-function mutations in MHC molecules. Overexpressing MHC/neoantigens (B) or increasing antigen presentation (C) would enhance, not evade, recognition, and permanent apoptosis (D) is not an evasion strategy. T cells in turn shape this Darwinian selection of immune-evasive subclones.
hy: Immune evasion: down-regulate antigen presentation, dedifferentiate, mutate MHC.

stem: Which molecule is the principal regulator of tumor angiogenesis, and what triggers its production?

options.A: VEGF, produced by cancer cells in response to hypoxia and acting on VEGFR2 on endothelial cells; tumor vessels are abnormal, leaky, and poorly perfused
options.B: Insulin, produced in response to hyperglycemia
options.C: E-cadherin, produced in response to normoxia
options.D: Caspase 9, produced in response to DNA damage

exp: VEGF is the principal regulator of tumor angiogenesis; cancer cells produce it in response to hypoxia, and it acts on VEGFR2 on endothelial cells to initiate neoangiogenesis — but the resulting vessels are structurally abnormal, leaky, and poorly perfused, sustaining chronic hypoxia. Insulin (B), E-cadherin (C), and caspase 9 (D) are not angiogenic regulators.
hy: Hypoxia → VEGF → VEGFR2 → abnormal, leaky tumor vessels.

stem: The "seed and soil" hypothesis of organ-specific metastasis is attributed to whom, and what does it propose?

options.A: Stephen Paget; metastatic organ tropism reflects favorable interactions between cancer cells ("seed") and specific organ microenvironments ("soil")
options.B: Rudolf Virchow; metastasis is purely random
options.C: Otto Warburg; metastasis depends only on glycolysis
options.D: Thomas Hunt Morgan; metastasis follows Notch mutations

exp: Stephen Paget proposed the "seed and soil" hypothesis: the organ preference of metastasis reflects favorable interactions between metastatic cancer cells ("seed") and the unique microenvironments ("soil") of particular organs. Virchow (B, irritation theory of cancer), Warburg (C, glycolysis), and Morgan (D, Notch/fruit-fly wing notching) made other contributions.
hy: Paget's "seed and soil" explains organ-specific metastasis.

stem: Which adhesion-molecule switch is considered a critical step initiating carcinoma invasion and migration, and what enzymes degrade the matrix?

options.A: A switch from E-cadherin to N-cadherin (epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition); matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) disassemble the extracellular matrix
options.B: A switch from N-cadherin to E-cadherin, with collagen synthases building matrix
options.C: Loss of all cadherins, with no enzymatic matrix change
options.D: Up-regulation of E-cadherin to promote invasion

exp: A switch from E-cadherin to N-cadherin (epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition) is regarded as a critical step initiating carcinoma invasion and migration, and matrix metalloproteinases (MMPs) — aided by inflammatory cells — disassemble the extracellular matrix to facilitate invasive growth. Option B reverses the cadherin switch, C ignores the MMP role, and D (up-regulating E-cadherin) would inhibit invasion.
hy: EMT: E-cadherin → N-cadherin switch; MMPs degrade the ECM.

stem: What is "anoikis," and how do metastasizing cells handle it?

options.A: An apoptosis-like death of cells that have lost cell–matrix contact; metastasis-initiating cells dynamically adapt adhesion networks to resist it while circulating
options.B: A form of uncontrolled proliferation triggered by matrix loss
options.C: The fusion of two cancer cells into one
options.D: A protective senescence state that prevents metastasis

exp: Anoikis is an apoptosis-like death triggered when cells lose their cell–matrix contact; metastasis-initiating cells dynamically adapt their adhesion networks to resist anoikis and survive in the circulation. It is not matrix-loss-triggered proliferation (B), not cell fusion (C), and not a protective senescence (D).
hy: Anoikis = death from lost matrix contact; metastatic cells resist it.

stem: Which statement about metastasis-initiating cells and the timing of driver mutations is correct?

options.A: They display a stem-like, plastic phenotype, can remain dormant in perivascular niches, and the majority of oncogenic driver mutations occur before metastatic dissemination
options.B: All driver mutations are acquired only after metastasis
options.C: Metastasis-initiating cells are terminally differentiated and non-plastic
options.D: Dormancy is impossible; disseminated cells either grow immediately or die

exp: Metastasis-initiating cells display the stem-like, plastic phenotype of their lineage progenitors, can remain dormant for long periods within perivascular niches before outgrowth, and — importantly — the majority of oncogenic driver mutations are acquired before metastatic dissemination (with chromosomal instability/copy-number changes accumulating later). They are not differentiated/non-plastic (C), drivers are not all post-metastatic (B), and dormancy does occur (D).
hy: Metastasis-initiating cells: stem-like, dormant in perivascular niches; most drivers precede metastasis.

stem: Which statement about keratinocyte carcinomas is correct?

options.A: They carry the highest mutational burden of any cancer type (UV signature); TP53 mutations are present in essentially all of them; BCCs are locally destructive and rarely metastasize, whereas cSCCs more often metastasize and cause most keratinocyte-carcinoma deaths
options.B: They have the lowest mutational burden of any cancer; TP53 is rarely involved
options.C: BCCs metastasize more often than cSCCs
options.D: cSCCs are locally destructive but essentially never metastasize

exp: Keratinocyte carcinomas carry the highest mutational burden of any cancer type with a UV-damage signature, and TP53 mutations are present in essentially all of them; clinically, BCCs are locally destructive but rarely metastasize, whereas cSCCs are less common but more likely to metastasize and cause most keratinocyte-carcinoma deaths. The other options invert the mutational burden (B), the metastatic propensity (C), or deny cSCC metastasis (D).
hy: KCs: highest mutational burden, TP53 in all; BCC rarely metastasizes, cSCC causes most KC deaths.

stem: Genetic studies of basal cell nevus syndrome (Gorlin syndrome) first implicated which gene/pathway in BCC, and how often is that pathway activated in sporadic BCC?

options.A: PTCH1 (a Hedgehog-pathway inhibitor) as a tumor suppressor; the Hedgehog pathway is activated in ~85% of BCCs
options.B: NOTCH1 as an oncogene; the Notch pathway is activated in ~85% of BCCs
options.C: BRAF; the MAPK pathway is activated in ~85% of BCCs
options.D: VEGF; angiogenesis is activated in ~85% of BCCs

exp: Linkage studies in basal cell nevus (Gorlin) syndrome identified PTCH1 — an inhibitor of the Hedgehog pathway — as a tumor suppressor inactivated in affected patients, and loss-of-function PTCH1 is also frequent in sporadic BCCs; overall the Hedgehog pathway is activated in about 85% of BCCs. NOTCH1 (B), BRAF/MAPK (C), and VEGF/angiogenesis (D) are not the Gorlin-identified BCC driver.
hy: Gorlin syndrome → PTCH1 (Hedgehog); Hedgehog activated in ~85% of BCCs.
src: Bologna Ch 107, "Molecular Pathogenesis of BCC" / Fig 107.9

stem: Beyond Hedgehog activation, which additional driver alterations contribute to BCC and its aggressive growth?

options.A: Mutations in TP53, the Notch pathway, N-Myc (MYCN), and Hippo-YAP — combined Hedgehog + Notch + N-Myc alterations drive aggressive BCC growth
options.B: Activating mutations in E-cadherin and VEGFR2 only
options.C: Loss of HRAS as the sole additional driver
options.D: Amplification of PTCH1 and SMO together

exp: Beyond Hedgehog activation, BCCs carry mutations in TP53, the Notch pathway, N-Myc (MYCN), and the Hippo-YAP pathway; experimentally, combined activation of Hedgehog and Notch signaling with N-Myc overexpression drives aggressive, unrestrained BCC growth, consistent with multistep cancer evolution. Activating E-cadherin/VEGFR2 (B), isolated HRAS loss (C), and PTCH1/SMO amplification (D) are not the described additional drivers.
hy: BCC drivers beyond Hedgehog: TP53, Notch, N-Myc (MYCN), Hippo-YAP.

stem: What is the role of Notch signaling in cutaneous squamous cell carcinoma, and how frequent are NOTCH1 mutations?

options.A: Notch acts as a tumor suppressor in skin (it supports keratinocyte differentiation), so loss-of-function Notch mutations are oncogenic; NOTCH1 is mutated in roughly half of cSCCs (~55%), the second most frequent driver group after TP53/Rb
options.B: Notch is an oncogene in skin, so activating NOTCH1 mutations drive cSCC
options.C: NOTCH1 mutations are absent in cSCC
options.D: Notch loss is protective against cSCC

exp: In skin, Notch signaling acts as a tumor suppressor because it supports keratinocyte differentiation, so loss-of-function Notch mutations are oncogenic in cSCC; NOTCH1 is mutated in roughly half of cSCCs (~55%), making the Notch pathway the second most frequent driver group after the p53–Rb pathway. Notch is not oncogenic in skin (B), NOTCH1 mutations are common not absent (C), and Notch loss promotes rather than protects against cSCC (D).
hy: Notch is a skin tumor suppressor; NOTCH1 loss-of-function in ~55% of cSCC (2nd after TP53/Rb).
src: Bologna Ch 107, "Molecular Pathogenesis of cSCC" / Fig 107.10

stem: The classic two-stage chemical-carcinogenesis mouse model of cSCC (e.g. DMBA) identified which initiating oncogenic mutation, and which cooperating loss enabled genomic instability?

options.A: Oncogenic HRAS mutations initiate; loss-of-function p53 mutations then enable chromosomal alterations and genomic instability
options.B: Oncogenic PTCH1 mutations initiate; gain of p16 enables instability
options.C: NOTCH1 amplification initiates; loss of VEGF enables instability
options.D: BRAF V600E initiates; gain of E-cadherin enables instability

exp: In the classic two-stage chemical-carcinogenesis mouse model, oncogenic HRAS mutations (induced by carcinogens such as DMBA) initiate cSCC, and subsequent loss-of-function p53 mutations enable chromosomal alterations and genomic instability that drive progression — findings later confirmed in human cSCC. Options B, C, and D pair the wrong initiating mutation and the wrong cooperating event.
hy: Two-stage cSCC model: HRAS initiates, p53 loss enables genomic instability.
